title: THE OS — Run the Machine You Built
tagline: The system is 78% ready and the clock isn't ticking. ~45 min to make the OS a habit, not an artifact.
fin_text: The OS runs on rhythm now. Two weeks of standup + close-day and you'll never go back.

levels.title: LEVEL 0 · THE DIAGNOSIS
levels.html:
You built a great OS and don't boot it

The audit's inventory: nucleus ✅ working · standup/close-day/batch/repurpose skills designed but not invoked in 30 days · agentbus ledger trusted by no one (1/19 done). The failure mode isn't design — it's that the clock never ticks. An OS without a scheduler is files.

What 'using the OS' actually means

Three loops, that's all: the day loop (standup → work → close-day), the week loop (Sunday batch → all-week distribution), the balance protocol (what happens when a shiny idea lands mid-commitment). Habit beats architecture. This course installs the habits.

quiz.q: The audit says the OS fails because…
opts: The clock never ticks — skills exist but aren't daily habits
quiz.ex: Every component exists and most work. What's missing is invocation: standup wasn't run, close-day wasn't run, batch never fired. Execution, not architecture.

mission.txt: Run the standup RIGHT NOW (say 'standup' to Claude). Day loop, iteration one. That's the whole mission.

levels.title: LEVEL 1 · THE DAY LOOP
levels.html:
Morning: standup (5 min)

Claude assembles it — you don't. One screen: yesterday's carryover, each active venture's top 1–3, ONE next action visible. Money Block first (Sales course). You approve or trade, then go.

Evening: close-day (5 min)

What shipped, what slipped and why, what carries. The why is the gold: it's where patterns (gates, posting stalls, scope creep) become visible instead of vibes. Close-day writes the carryover that makes tomorrow's standup honest.

The streak IS the system

Ten minutes a day total. The audit's proof-of-success: standup + close-day daily for 2 weeks → measure commitment-match (what you said vs. what shipped). The number will be embarrassing in week 1 and motivating in week 2.

quiz.q: What's the most valuable part of close-day?
opts: The WHY behind slipped items — that's where your patterns become visible
quiz.ex: Slippage-why is the diagnostic feed: 'waited on a gate', 'froze on posting', 'new idea ate the slot'. Two weeks of whys = your personal friction map, with receipts.

mission.txt: Tonight, run close-day ('close out' to Claude). Then commit to the 14-day streak: both loops daily. The streak counter goes in the nucleus.

levels.title: LEVEL 2 · THE WEEK LOOP
levels.html:
Create once, distribute all week

The batch system (Sun/Mon 2–4h raw creation → 'repurpose' atomizes into clips/posts/emails → board queues the week) is built and was never fired for SSA. One batch session feeds five days of posting — it's the ADHD-correct shape: one deep session instead of daily blank pages.

The board is the single pane

'os-board' = command center: context, in-flight, done, agent roster. Trust rule from the Automation course applies: a clean board you check beats a perfect board you avoid.

quiz.q: Why does batching beat daily content creation for you specifically?
opts: One deep session replaces five blank-page starts — blank pages are where ADHD stalls
quiz.ex: The blank page is the highest-friction moment, and daily posting schedules five of them a week. Batch scheduling buys five posting days for ONE starting-cost.

mission.txt: Book the 2-hour batch block on your real calendar (Sunday or Monday) and tell Claude the slot — the 'batch' skill runs it, SSA content is the agenda.

levels.title: LEVEL 3 · THE BALANCE PROTOCOL (priority, for real)
levels.html:
The 52/2 problem

Miklat took 52% of the month; the money-maker took 2%. Not because you chose that — because shippable beats important when no protocol intervenes. The balance protocol exists for exactly this; it just needs to actually fire.

The 2-box rule, every standup

  • Box 1 — Money-maker: SSA/BYB. Eats first (the Money Block). Non-negotiable.
  • Box 2 — Long bet: Miklat (or whatever is hot). Gets the rest, guilt-free.

When a new idea lands mid-week: capture it (nucleus), state today's commitment, offer the trade explicitly. Never a flat no — and never a silent yes that buries Box 1.

Session separation

Miklat lives in the miklatgames session; the portfolio lives in dubaiai-pro. Separate dopamine tracks — opening the right session IS the priority decision.

quiz.q: A shiny new idea lands on Tuesday mid-commitment. The protocol says…
opts: Do it now, ride the energy
Capture it, state today's commitment, offer the explicit trade
quiz.ex: Energy is real and worth honoring — through a TRADE, not a hijack. 'Captured. Today is committed to X. Want to swap X out, eyes open?' keeps both the spark and the spine.

mission.txt: Declare this week's two boxes to Claude (Box 1: SSA first booking · Box 2: Miklat portal submissions — or your own). They anchor every standup this week.

levels.title: LEVEL 4 · DEFINITION OF DONE + BOSS
levels.html:
One rule, applied everywhere

You already coined it for content: drafted ≠ done, published = done. The audit shows the gap is portfolio-wide: site designed ≠ site live · video scripted ≠ video posted · email drafted ≠ email sent · fix merged ≠ fix deployed · agent built ≠ agent running. Nothing counts until a stranger can touch it.

Done-washing is the OS's silent killer

Every 'basically done' item on the board inflates your sense of progress and deflates the trust the standup runs on. The close-day question is always: can a stranger touch it? No → it carries, honestly.

boss.q: The OS's actual missing piece was…
opts: The daily clock: standup + close-day as habits
Money-maker eats first; long bet gets the rest guilt-free

boss.q: Mid-week shiny idea → the protocol response is…

opts: Commitment-match: what you said vs. what shipped

mission.txt: Sweep the board with Claude using the stranger-test: relabel everything 'basically done' to its true state. Then start the 14-day streak. Day 1 is today.
